title: iOS 27 Hands-On: Top 5 New Features!

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: All right, it's time to talk about iOS 18, coming to an iPhone near you.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So I've been running the latest beta of iOS 18 for a little while now, since WWDC basically. The public beta is out now too, which means you can test it as well. And this is the update of refinement. Like, there are a lot of—there was no big sweeping changes. There's a lot of little things in here that we've been waiting for for a long time, and you'll see what I mean.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: What's crazy is that this has been among the most stable, like the best set of developer betas I've tested in a long time from Apple. Not that that really means anything for the everyday person who's going to wait until September when it officially drops on everyone's phones, but it's a good sign. It's just, it's nice to see from where I'm sitting.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Anyway, this is the top five features of iOS 18 in my order of how good they are. Also, if you haven't already gotten subscribed to this channel, make sure you get subscribed to be among the first to see the new iPhones that this launches on when that comes out in a few short months.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So number five is the new photo editing features. So in the Photos app now, when you open a photo and you go to edit, there's two new tools. Cleanup was already there for erasing background items and it's actually gotten noticeably better. But now there's also two new ones: Extend and Reframe.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I've been playing with these a lot. Extend is pretty intuitive to me. It's basically going to use generative AI to extend the edges of a photo, no matter what camera it was taken on, in any direction. And it seems maybe a bit silly at first, but this is actually a pretty commonly used tool, at least for me in Photoshop, when I just want to change the aspect ratio of something. Maybe I shot something in portrait but I want to make it landscape for this video instead. Okay, you can just extend and pinch out. And it does a pretty great job of reconstructing more of whatever was on the outer edges of the frame.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Obviously it works best with more consistent patterns and abstract stuff, but it's also been shockingly good with whole entire objects too, just sort of creating more of it from scratch.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But definitely the crazier one that we haven't seen before is this spatial reframe feature. So this one is going to let you drag around and morph the perspective from which it appears a photo was taken. So it's like kind of being able to move where the camera was in physical space. And then it takes some time and generates all these new pixels that it wasn't originally able to see. And yeah, it does it.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now I would say the quality of these reframes isn't quite as good, like it definitely degenerates a bit more quickly than the extends. But it is kind of insane that it works and that Apple's just letting you do this to your photos. I think there's clearly a little bit less practical uses for this spatial reframe. It's a very cool tech demo, but I do think it's kind of convenient for the stuff where you're trying to like line it up perfectly, like one of those perfectly symmetrical shots where you kind of just get the snap like 96% right in person and then you want to post it but you want it to be perfect so you get those last few percent by reframing it and getting it just right.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Is that still a photo that you took with your phone? In my opinion, no. But, uh, who's counting?

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Also, it will not let you extend or reframe the same photo more than once. So it'll let you do it once and then says, "Alright, that's enough." But you can also sort of screenshot it and get your way around it and hack your way into multiple extends and multiple reframes.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Also, both of these features require an internet connection. So if you try to do this while you're offline or in an airplane, then it will give you an error message because you need those bigger models on the cloud to actually pull this off.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: All right. So number four is what I'm calling the "freaking finally" section, because it's about time. Each one of these things was like Apple stubbornly refusing to do it for some reason to the point where it became a meme. Like, it's been years.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But now, for iOS 27, they've found the time. So finally, separate alarm volume from the rest of system volume in iOS 27.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Freaking finally, bro. This has been an Android feature for like 20 years at this point. And honestly, I still think Android manages this better with separate volume toggles, and it makes it very clear. But hey, at this point, I'll take it. I don't care. It's about time.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Also, better management of switching off of weak Wi-Fi networks back to cellular. This is something I've dealt with for years. I'm sure a lot of other people have had this. You're leaving your house and you pull up Maps to like navigate to work or wherever and just want to see traffic.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: For like the first two blocks, every single time. I have like a tree that I know that I have to get to where it's not going to know what the instructions are. It's not going to know the traffic until I pass it, it gets off of my house's Wi-Fi, and then it connects to cellular.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But I've noticed it actually being better on iOS 27. Just leaving, just going straight to cellular.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: In CarPlay and iOS 27, in "Now Playing," you can finally scrub the media back and forth that you're actually playing on the timeline.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Finally! What's taking so long? And then AirPods are finally getting custom EQ.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: What? Finally! At this point, it's been so long I was convinced they were holding back on this on purpose for some weird reason. But okay, it's here now.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: At least for AirPods with an H2 chip or later. And it's only a three-band equalizer, but still, it's crazy to see. I'll take it. It's about time.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I don't know who had to lose a bet over there for this stuff to happen, but thank you for losing that bet. It's about time. I appreciate it. Moving on.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So number three is the aesthetic tweaks. Because there are a ton of little aesthetic improvements everywhere throughout this update. Obviously iOS 26 was the big one with liquid glass.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: That's not going anywhere. But right from the jump on iOS 27, you can see like the home screen, the icons are just...

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: you can tell they're a little bit different. The text and the shadows are just a little bit easier to read, they're a little crisper.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: If you look at Apple's stock apps, they've actually updated a couple of their icons.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And they've actually finally caved and added a new Appearances tab, and in this tab, right at the top, is a literal s...

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Slider to dial in exactly how much liquid glass you actually want. By default, it's in the middle, which I've found is just a tiny bit more opaque than before. But then you can go all the way to the right, which makes it way more opaque and frosted, or all the way to the left, which makes it way more glassy and transparent for you real liquid glass sickos out there. It's basic stuff, but I definitely appreciate it. I think a lot of you guys will, too.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Also, on the lock screen, there's this new compact clock option if you just want to shrink that thing all the way down.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But they've also completely redesigned the home screen swipe down search, which is now combined with Siri.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Which conveniently brings me to number two, which is the new Siri, aka Siri AI.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So, okay, I would say the bar was very low for Siri before. Like, the last 10 years of Siri, it's been borderline useless, I would say. Uh, probably have been more accidental triggers of Siri than intentional ones.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But now with the help of Google, it's gotten this complete overhaul from the ground up. It starts with this new animation, so you can hold down the power button like before and it expands out of the Dynamic Island and it's this half-see-through, half-opaque sort of crystal orb thing that moves as you talk.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then once it pops up your answer as an overlay, you can actually drag the bottom down to turn it into this full overlay conversation window. And then you can even full screen it to open it alone in its brand new standalone Siri app. So it goes from being an overlay to being in the Siri app. And this app actually does keep a history of your requests. It's not every single request, but it's most of them and, you know, keeps the bigger ones that you might want to revisit.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It also has a new voice that is much more expressive and human-like and is actually customizable. I appreciate that you can make it talk faster.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Hi, let's find the right voice for you. Hi. Hi, let's find the right voice for you. Hi, hi, let's find the right voice for you. Hi, let's find the right voice for you. Let's find the right voice for you. Hi, let's find the right voice for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So that's pretty sweet. And yeah, it just generally feels like they've caught up to the basic functionality of say Gemini on an Android phone. It's more conversational now, so you can ask it like follow-up questions about something and it will retain that context and keep it going. Uh, it has broad world knowledge that it actually uses now instead of just saying, 'Hey, I looked this up for you' or 'I Googled it.' And it has on-screen awareness now, so you can ask it things about whatever you're looking at on your iPhone. As long as you use the right language, because there's no UI to get it to look at the screen. But I'd say its biggest new capability is being able to dig into the data and the personal context that's actually on your phone. So I'm talking about like your iMessages, your emails, your calendar events, and your photos. So when you first get this update, it actually indexes in the background for a while. It'll say it's optimizing for the new Siri. For me, this was a couple of days actually, but once that's over, you can ask it things like 'What was that watch Dave sent me?'

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Dave Lee sent you a message about the light meter watch, LMWV1 from Kickstarter. He mentioned that he thought David Imel and Brandon would like it.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: See that's coming straight from my messages. Or, where was I two Saturdays ago?

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Two Saturdays ago, on June 27th, 2026, you were in Greensboro, North Carolina. You stayed at a hotel in a rural area and mentioned playing in a game that day.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So if you pull down, you can see this is pulling from my wallet transactions, my messages, and it seems like geotags from photos I took on those days. So it's pretty comprehensive and it actually lets you dig up stuff from your personal history as long as it's gone through one of these Apple apps.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It's similar to what Google is doing with Gemini, which is going to the cloud and pulling from your Google services like Gmail or Google Calendar, but in my opinion this one's better. It's faster because it's locally indexed and it's a much more visual display.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And it's also multimodal. So, like I said, it can answer visual questions about what's on your screen. But also, they just added a new mode to the camera app. It's one swipe away and it just says Siri. So you can now take a photo in Siri mode and then ask Siri about that image.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So kind of like what you might have done with Google Lens a few years ago. It's honestly pretty good. I'd say it's caught up to what you'd say is like the base expected functionality from an AI assistant on your phone in 2026. It's no longer a cellar-dwelling shadow of an AI assistant.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now here's the thing about this new Siri. Uh, no third-party app ties yet. So this is the thing I was talking about in my early coverage of it which is like, okay, of course it's going to work best if you're using a bunch of Apple services: iMessage, Apple photos, Apple calendar, all that stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Like when I asked about the texts with Dave, that's from iMessage, right? But what about my conversations I've had in WhatsApp or Telegram or Gmail or any of these other things? It doesn't know anything about any of those conversations.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So my understanding now is basically that these developers or these app developers will be able to push an update to their app that allows Siri to look into their data and basically find stuff that is flagged for Siri to find it specifically. So things like third-party messaging apps will be able to tell Siri, 'hey, this is more messages here' and other things like calendars and even a bunch more other types of apps.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And that is really cool to me. There's even little tiny hints of them starting to trickle out now like when I ask 'What's the battery status of my car?', it then actually asks me the user if I want to enable Siri to look into these other apps and so I selected one and when I said yes to Porsche's, it was able to tell me.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Although you can see Rivian's app is right there on my home screen with the widget, but that doesn't show up in the list for me, so clearly they haven't pushed an update yet. Basically now it's a waiting game to see how many of these developers will actually update their apps to let Siri look into their data. And I think we'll probably start to see a lot more of that stuff around.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: September, when, you know, the new iPhones come out and everybody gets iOS 27 at once, but for now, it's a TBD on how much this sort of access level levels up Siri to being this little agent that crawls around your phone and finds a bunch of stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Also, in the comments under the like button, let me know if you want a review just on the new Siri against like Gemini on Android, because I think there is a lot more that I could talk about that I have opinions on from like sourcing to how it displays data and when it shows you your transcriptions and the UI and all this other stuff, but that—that's a whole separate thing, let me know what you think. I just—for now, Siri's pretty good. So that's why it's my number two feature.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But real quick before the number one, I also have some honorable mentions, some things that I would like to show you but that didn't quite make my top five. So, if you open a picture of food in the new Photos app in iOS 27, Siri will not just identify that it is food, which it could already do, but then it also surfaces a look up nutrition button that will tell you on a sliding scale of how healthy it thinks that food is. Nothing super detailed like trying to guess calorie counts or anything like that, but yeah, I guess that's a potentially interesting information when it's accurate.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: The passwords app now also can do the thing where it goes through and agentically automatically logs in and fixes all of your weak or compromised passwords, and then I guess makes you an official passwords app relier. Insane. Uh, you can also describe a brand new Siri shortcut with natural language and it will make it for you from scratch and let you edit it after the fact. That is incredible. And you can also now do threaded replies in iMessage with Android users. Finally.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But the number one best new thing in iOS 27 is that everything is noticeably faster. So this is crazy. I know they said a bunch of like huge crazy numbers on stage like 70% faster photo loading into the gallery after you take a picture, or 30% faster app loading across the board, or 80% faster AirDrops, just like massive changes.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And the numbers on stage are one thing, but actually using the phone and feeling it be noticeably smoother and snappier is actually pretty sick. Now, okay, on a brand new iPhone 16 or 17 or 18, this is a bit more subtle obviously, I feel like that's already a pretty fast phone. I do notice the faster AirDrops, but where this really shines is older phones. Because for all that talk about iOS updates like slowing down your phone, it's pretty refreshing to see compatibility for this software go all the way back to iPhone 11 back from 2019. And with this new optimized CPU scheduler, it's really kicking in and making an even bigger difference for this older phone to feel much newer again. And I'm personally all here for an update that doesn't have to make many visual changes but actually addresses a lot of the behind-the-scenes bloat and inefficiencies to just make everything run that much more smoothly.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So there you have it, iOS 27. I think when this update hits your phone, download it, install it, do it. I know that there's real people that hit me up when...

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Software updates come out they're like i don't i don't really want to install this one i feel like it's going to mess things up but

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: this one you should you should install it

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Do it.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Thanks for watching.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Catch you in the next one.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Peace.

highlights: The update introduces generative AI photo tools including "Extend," which expands image edges to change aspect ratios, and "Spatial Reframe," which allows users to shift the perceived camera perspective after a photo is taken.

Siri has been completely overhauled with a new conversational interface and "on-screen awareness," allowing it to pull personal context from messages, emails, and calendar events to answer complex requests.
Apple addressed several long-standing user requests, finally adding separate alarm volume controls, a three-band equalizer for AirPods, and the ability to scrub media timelines in CarPlay.
System-wide performance optimizations provide noticeably faster app loading and smoother animations, offering a significant speed boost that revitalizes older hardware like the iPhone 11.
summary:
In this transcript, Marques Brownlee reviews the latest developer beta of iOS 18, characterizing it as an update defined by refinement, stability, and the long-awaited arrival of "common sense" features. He highlights a category of "freaking finally" additions—such as separate alarm volume controls, custom EQ for AirPods, and improved Wi-Fi to cellular switching—that address long-standing user complaints. Additionally, the update introduces significant aesthetic tweaks and generative AI photo tools, including an "Extend" feature to fill in image borders and a "Spatial Reframe" tool that morphs the camera's original perspective.

The most substantial changes center on a complete overhaul of Siri and overall system performance. The new "Siri AI" is now more conversational and multimodal, capable of "seeing" the screen and indexing personal data across Apple apps to answer context-specific questions about messages, emails, and calendar events. However, Brownlee ranks the update’s speed optimizations as the number one feature; improved CPU scheduling significantly enhances the snappiness of the UI and the speed of app loading. He notes that these performance gains are particularly impressive on older hardware, making the update highly recommended for all users upon its official release.

First look at iOS 27 - coming soon to an iPhone near you. It's about time.

topics: New AI-powered photo editing tools like Extend and Reframe
Long-awaited system utility improvements and software features
Aesthetic and user interface customization options
The overhaul of Siri with AI and personal context integration
System-wide performance optimizations and speed improvements
